passages.explanation: When Jesus finishes these words, he departs from Galilee and comes to the borders of Judea beyond the Jordan. Great multitudes follow him, and he heals them. Pharisees come testing him: is it lawful for a man to divorce his wife for any reason? Jesus answers by going back to Genesis: haven't you read that he who made them from the beginning made them male and female, and said a man shall leave his father and mother and be joined to his wife, and the two shall become one flesh? What God has joined together, don't let man tear apart. They press: why then did Moses command giving a certificate of divorce? Jesus says: Moses permitted divorce because of the hardness of your hearts, but from the beginning it wasn't so. Whoever divorces his wife, except for sexual immorality, and marries another, commits adultery. Jesus doesn't engage the rabbinic debate about acceptable grounds — he reframes the entire question around God's original design.

The disciples say: if that's the case with a man and his wife, it's better not to marry! Jesus replies: not everyone can receive this saying, but those to whom it is given. Some are eunuchs from birth, some were made eunuchs by men, and some have made themselves eunuchs for the Kingdom of Heaven's sake. He who can receive it, let him receive it. Jesus acknowledges that both marriage and singleness are valid callings. Neither is compulsory. Both require grace.

Little children are brought to Jesus so he can lay hands on them and pray. The disciples rebuke them. Jesus says: allow the little children to come to me, and don't forbid them — for the Kingdom of Heaven belongs to such as these. He lays his hands on them and departs. The disciples keep trying to manage access to Jesus. He keeps overruling them in favour of the overlooked.

A man comes and asks: good teacher, what good thing shall I do that I may have eternal life? Jesus says: why do you call me good? No one is good but one — God. If you want to enter into life, keep the commandments. Which ones? Jesus lists them: don't murder, don't commit adultery, don't steal, don't bear false witness, honour your father and mother, love your neighbour as yourself. The young man says: I've kept all these. What do I still lack? Jesus tells him: if you want to be perfect, go, sell what you have, give to the poor, and you'll have treasure in heaven. Then come, follow me. The young man goes away sorrowful, because he has great possessions. Jesus doesn't prescribe poverty for everyone — he identifies the one thing this man won't surrender. The commandments this man claims to have kept are all relational. The one he can't keep is about letting go.

Jesus tells his disciples: most certainly I say to you, it's hard for a rich man to enter the Kingdom of Heaven. It's easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich man to enter God's Kingdom. The disciples are greatly astonished: who then can be saved? Jesus looks at them and says: with men this is impossible, but with God all things are possible. The disciples are shocked because wealth was widely seen as a sign of God's blessing. If the blessed can't get in, who can? Jesus' answer: no one — without God.

Peter says: we've left everything and followed you. What then will we have? Jesus tells them: in the regeneration, when the Son of Man sits on the throne of his glory, you who have followed me will also sit on twelve thrones, judging the twelve tribes of Israel. Everyone who has left houses, brothers, sisters, father, mother, wife, children, or lands for my name's sake will receive a hundred times as much, and will inherit eternal life. But many who are first will be last, and the last first. The promise isn't that sacrifice goes unrewarded — it's that the reward is disproportionate to the cost. But the final line is a warning: don't assume your current position guarantees your future one.
